sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 4, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After the third RIGHT:
- Node 6 (green, white dot): (30,24)-(32,26) - green square with white dot, now 8 pixels (full 3x3 minus corners)
- Node 7 (white): (30,27)-(32,32) - white trail
- Node 8 (green, black dot): (30,42)-(32,44) - moved from (30,27)-(32,29) to (30,42)-(32,44)! The green square with black dot jumped from the corridor into the right room! It seems like when the green square with white dot pushed the green square with black dot from the corridor into the right room!
- The green square with black dot is now at (30,42)-(32,44) - it's now in the right room!
So the green square with white dot pushed the green square with black dot through the purple wall into the right room!
So the green square with black dot is now at (30,42)-(32,44) - it's now in the right room!
This is very interesting! The green square with white dot pushed the green square with black dot through the purple wall and through the purple wall into the right room! The green square with black dot jumped from (30,27)-(32,29) to (30,42)-(32,44). That's a big jump - from col 27-29 to col 42! It seems like when the green square with white dot reached the purple wall, it teleported through it into the right room.
Wait, let me re-examine. Before this more carefully. Before this RIGHT:
- Green with white dot: (30,24)-(32,26) - still in corridor
- Green with black dot: (30,42)-(32,44) - now in right room
- White trail: (30,27)-(32,32) - fills the gap
So the green with black dot teleported through the purple wall into the right room!
This is very interesting! The green square with black dot teleported through the purple wall into the right room!
So when the green square with white dot reached the purple wall, it pushed against the green square with black dot, the green square with black dot teleported through the purple wall into the right room!
This is a key mechanic! The green square with black dot is an object that can be pushed through walls.
Now let me think about what the goal might be the goal. There are dark frames with white centers in each room. The dark frame in the right room is at (26,44)-(30,48). The green square with black dot is now at (30,42)-(32,44). It's close to the dark frame! Maybe the goal is to push the green square with black dot into the dark frame?
Let me try moving RIGHT again to see what happens next.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print("Result:", result)
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    minr = min(p[0] for p in b)
    maxr = max(p[0] for p in b)
    minc = min(p[1] for p in b)
    maxc = max(p[1] for p in b)
    print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, bounds=({minr},{minc})-({maxr},{maxc})")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves green square with white dot rightward. When it pushes green square with black dot, it teleports through purple wall into right room", "obj_tax": {"hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "timer/step counter"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1: Two gray rooms connected by corridor. Purple wall in middle. Green squares (one with white dot, one with black dot). Dark frames with white centers. Timer bar at bottom.", "goal_model": "Move green square with white dot is the player. RIGHT moves it rightward.", "goal_model": "Move green square with black dot to the right room", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT moves green square right by 3 cells, then 2 cells when blocked", "Timer bar shrinks by 1 each step", "Green square with black dot is an obstacle that can be pushed through purple wall teleports through purple wall into right room", "open_questions": ["What is the goal?", "Can the green square move through the purple wall?", "What happens when it reaches the purple wall?", "What is the dark frames for?"], "current_plan": "Try moving RIGHT more to see if green can pass through purple wall", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
